author: @Gwydion67
text:
Memories... some  40 years ago. New Year's fireworks, selfmade.
Not daring to meddle with the real exploding stuff, I turned on the "deflagrating" ones. Selfmade a vulcano from pottery with an inset for the mixtures. Tried any coloring salts as ingredients. I still have the smoke in mind...
After school, I completed an apprenticeship at Shell and work in the profession for some time, even went to technical college, although not finishing.
Well, that's life... Nevertheless, memories I won't miss! 🥰🔥🎆🧨

author: @threeMetreJim
text: Buy potassium nitrate, end up on watch list... Normally you'd grind the potassium nitrate separately, rather than with the fuel to prevent any accidents. Hopefully no one does a really big one and ends up with a fuel/oxidiser mix in a confined space - maybe it will smoke a lot, maybe it will do something else.

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
You should have showed how you packed the fine powder into the tube. Also what you used and how much pressure is required. It is these simple yet important steps that make a project a success or a failure.
Never assume that it is a given, if it is a necessary step then the information to complete the step is equally as necessary.

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
A number of youtube videos have shown that the addition of a soft wax greatly increases the amount of smoke generated.
Flame is the enemy of smoke generators, because it burn up the smoke particles. For maximum smoke output the compound needs to burn as cool as possible, that mean a smoldering flame.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Hey thanks for the tip! I do have a few crayons laying around hahah.

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text:
@MyNameIsLukey the potassium nitrate, sugar and sodium bicarbonate ground separately, then  mix and stirred into melted paraffin wax as it cools. The resulting suspension is then poured into think walled cardboard tubes.   I really find it hard to  believe you have not seen the many YouTube videos on the subject. I have collected a number of them as references, even covering coloured and scented smoke generation and other smoke mixtures. I feel it would be unfair to post any links here, as you are about to produce your own on the subject. But there are some very good ones made by professional chemists as well as experienced amateurs. 

I am a little concerned about the availability of some of the chemicals in the uk. The authorities seem to take a very dim view of amateurs buying strong oxidising agents, such as potassium nitrate, and there are laws against making what amount to fireworks.
It is certainly unfortunate that such experimentation is now so frowned upon, but it is the result of a combination of terrorist acts and health and safety concerns following accidents. In my youth access to raw chemical feed stocks was not a problrm.  Control of such chemicals was far more lax, even to the point where potassium nitrate, potassium permanganate, sulphur, elemental iodine, and the three main mineral acids in dilution, and could be purchased at the local pharmacists, what was then known as the high street chemist shop.
 Chlorates and ammonium nitrate were readily available off the shelf in any garden centre, along with copper and iron sulphate, some even supplied phosphates. From the local hardware store, it was possible to buy solvents such as toluene, benzene and carbon tetrachloride. The sale if such chemicals have long since been banned. 
 Most practical chemistry videos now start with a warning, even those originating from countries with fewer regulation, such as the US and Australia, come with a disclaimer stating they are for educational purposes only, and should not be copied by the viewer. No doubt such disclaimers are to protect the originator from litigation, should the information provided is used unlawfully or  linked to a serious accident.
Searching the internet for chemistry videos, I am constantly amazed at the availability of off the shelf  chemicals in other countries to the UK. 
I am aware it is possible to buy such compounds over the internet, from places such as amazon and eBay, but you can bet that such purchases are monitored by the authorities, or are reported by the distributors. The fact that these chemicals are offered for sale is no guarantee that their possession is legal in any country without a licence or permit, this is particularly true of  the UK, which must have some of the strictest rules in the world.

author: @BoB4jjjjs
text:
I want colours!!!  I once set off a smoke bomb that I was handed at work "Get rid of this, take it out somewhere and set it off" was the instructions.  So I took it out into the countryside (where I stayed) and one calm night I lit the fuse and stood back to watch the 10 inch tin of smoke do its work.  OMG!  The whole place filled with smoke for miles, very thick, very dense smoke, there was no wind to take it away and it hung around for well into an hour.  It slowly drifted up into the sky in a tall white column and I was told later it could be seen from 6 miles away.  I found out later that it was for testing flues of a rather large size, around chimney size or industrial chimneys.  It kept going for 10 to 15 minutes and of course it was not going to stop until it had finished!  There was a helicopter came around and flew around for a while, I don't know if they were looking for the source of the smoke or it was just coincidence they were flying around the area.  I couldn't see anything around the area for smoke.  All I can say is thankfully it didn't have a strong smell.  I told my boss who had handed it to me and he was in hysterics when I told him.  I asked why he didn't warn me how much smoke it would put out, his reply "There is no fun in telling you that".  :-((  Just as well I didn't stay beside a busy road!
Don't tell anyone about it!  Sheeesh!
